content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Administration 
Jennifer Soloway 
Counsel 
Department of Housing and 
Community Development 
910 -
10th Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
•• 
Enforcement 
322·5901 
322·6441 
February 5, 1982 
Statements of Economic: Interest 
322-64.44 
Re: 
Telephone Advice Request 
Dear Ms. Soloway: 
Thank you for your telephone request for advice. 
This 
letter is to confirm my advice in response to your questions. 
You were calling on behalf of the Director of the Department. 
He has asked you whether there would be any potential conflict 
of interest arising if the Department hired a person as 
consultant to assist in preparation of an RFP (Request for 
Proposals) for the Century Freeway Project operated by the 
Department. 
The person under consideration has recently done consulting 
work for a private, for-profit developer, on a project which 
the developer is planning to submit under the RFP, once it 
is developed. 
As the consultant working on the RFP, he will 
be in a position to draft an RFP which would give a definite 
advantage to or otherwise affect the developer's project. 
In addition, he has had disc~ssions with the developer regarding 
possible future consulting work if the project gets funded. 
The developer will also be seeking funding from other sources. 
My advice was that under these circumstances it would 
appear that the developer has been a source of income to the 
prospective consultant of $250 or more in the last 12 months. 
A successful proposal resulting in an award to the developer 
would assuredly have a material financial effect on this 
source of income, distinguishable from its effect upon the 
public generally. 
Consequently, a conflict of interest 
would exist under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. 


Jennifer Soloway 
February 5, 1982 
Pa'je 'I\vo 
You raised two additional possibilities, (1) that the 
prospective consultant commit that he would not work further 
for the developer on the project or any other project of the 
developer for two years, (2) that the developer commit not 
to seek funding for any of its projects under any RFP developed 
for the Century Freeway Project from the work-product of the 
prospective consultant. 
The l~ter would remove any foreseeable 
material financial effect upo~ the developer as a source of 
income. 
The first would not accomplish this as long as the 
developer was a source of income to the prospective consultant 
of $250 or more during the preceding 12 months. 
I hope that this advice is of assistance to JOu and the 
Director. 
If you have further questions, please do not 
hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901. 
~EL:plh 
Sincerely, 
2tc,<-t;:~~;(1·~ ;,,1-
Robert E. 
~eidigh 
Counsel 
Legal Division

sections.question: Whether there would be any potential conflict of interest arising if the Department hired a person as consultant to assist in preparation of an RFP (Request for Proposals) for the Century Freeway Project operated by the Department. The person under consideration has recently done consulting work for a private, for-profit developer, on a project which the developer is planning to submit under the RFP, once it is developed. As the consultant working on the RFP, he will be in a position to draft an RFP which would give a definite advantage to or otherwise affect the developer's project. In addition, he has had discussions with the developer regarding possible future consulting work if the project gets funded.
sections.conclusion: A conflict of interest would exist under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. The developer has been a source of income to the prospective consultant of $250 or more in the last 12 months, and a successful proposal resulting in an award to the developer would assuredly have a material financial effect on this source of income, distinguishable from its effect upon the public generally. A commitment by the developer not to seek funding for any of its projects under any RFP developed for the Century Freeway Project from the work-product of the prospective consultant would remove any foreseeable material financial effect, but a commitment by the consultant not to work further for the developer for two years would not accomplish this.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a conflict of interest exist under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 if the Department hires a consultant who recently worked for a developer that will be submitting proposals under the RFP he would help draft, and who has discussed future consulting work with that developer?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A conflict of interest exists because the consultant's prior relationship with the developer creates a material financial interest that would be affected by the RFP decisions. Only a commitment from the developer to exclude its projects from the RFP would resolve the conflict; the consultant's commitment to avoid future work for the developer would not suffice.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Well-structured advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Document confirms telephone advice in writing. Two alternative remedial approaches are discussed, with FPPC analysis of effectiveness of each.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether there would be any potential conflict of interest arising if the Department hired a person as consultant to assist in preparation of an RFP (Request for Proposals) for the Century Freeway Project operated by the Department. The person under consideration has recently done consulting work for a private, for-profit developer, on a project which the developer is planning to submit under the RFP, once it is developed. As the consultant working on the RFP, he will be in a position to draft an RFP which would give a definite advantage to or otherwise affect the developer's project. In addition, he has had discussions with the developer regarding possible future consulting work if the project gets funded.

CONCLUSION: A conflict of interest would exist under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. The developer has been a source of income to the prospective consultant of $250 or more in the last 12 months, and a successful proposal resulting in an award to the developer would assuredly have a material financial effect on this source of income, distinguishable from its effect upon the public generally. A commitment by the developer not to seek funding for any of its projects under any RFP developed for the Century Freeway Project from the work-product of the prospective consultant would remove any foreseeable material financial effect, but a commitment by the consultant not to work further for the developer for two years would not accomplish this.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel advises that hiring a consultant to draft an RFP for the Century Freeway Project creates a conflict of interest if the consultant recently worked for a developer planning to submit proposals under that RFP and has discussed future work with them. The conflict can only be resolved if the developer commits not to seek funding under the RFP.
